MARKET.head: 15 Sommeliers Name Expensive Bordeaux Worth the Premium Price
MARKET.summ: Unlike Burgundy, where most of the region’s output is price prohibitive, Bordeaux boasts a vast number of approachable bottles with many falling within the $15 to $30 range. That said, some occasions call for a bank-breaking bottle, and Bordeaux is also well-equipped with expressions that come with three- and four-digit price tags.
takeaways: Château Palmer, a third-growth classified as a 'Super Second,' offers emotional complexity with violet and graphite notes that justify premium pricing.
Château Lafleur, a tiny family-run Pomerol estate, commands high prices but rewards buyers with power and elegance rooted in meticulous vineyard farming.
La Mission Haut-Brion delivers first-growth quality from Graves terroir at lower cost than its famous neighbor Haut-Brion across the road.
Château Pontet-Canet, a fifth growth using biodynamic farming, undercuts quality peers on price while maintaining purity of fruit and balanced structure.
Super Second estates like Ducru-Beaucaillou and Cos d'Estournel offer savory complexity and aging potential comparable to first growths at more accessible prices.

MARKET.head: Sicilia DOC unites fractious producers under single regional brand
MARKET.summ: Sicily isn’t Italy (you can ask anyone), and Sicilian wine isn’t Italian wine either. Many Sicilian producers have embraced the Sicilia DOC designation as a simple but effective identity. It’s the kind of idea that other wine regions might consider in today’s crowded marketplace.
takeaways: Sicilia DOC, established in 2011 after years of negotiation, simplifies consumer choice by offering a broad regional identity rather than competing sub-appellations like Etna DOC.
The designation struggled for adoption initially because producers preferred their own sub-regional brands, but now functions as an important force in Sicilian wine.
American tourism to Sicily, amplified by The White Lotus TV series and post-COVID travel, drives demand for specific Sicilian grapes like Grillo, Nero d'Avola, and Inzolia.
Sicilia DOC wines are now imported to the U.S., with Heritage Collection distributing Tasca d'Almerita bottlings at accessible price points.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, England's pioneer vineyard, sells for first time since 1974
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Peter Hall's Breaky Bottom near Lewes, Sussex, founded in 1974, listed at £4 million following Hall's death in October 2025.
The 15-acre estate produces around 10,000 bottles annually, focusing on sparkling wines from Seyval Blanc and Chardonnay-Pinot blends.
Hall's wines were served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and London 2012 Olympics.
Knight Frank expects international interest, citing resilient demand for small-scale, terroir-driven producers despite broader wine market challenges.
The sale reflects growing investor interest in English wine as 2025 production surged 39% to 16.5 million bottles.

MARKET.head: Babich Wines marks 110 years as New Zealand's rare family-owned producer
MARKET.summ: From a Croatian immigrant filling barrels by hand to exports in more than 60 countries, Babich Wines' 110-year journey reflects the rise of New Zealand wine itself
takeaways: Josip Babich, a Croatian immigrant, founded the winery in 1916 with hand-filled oak barrels; it now exports to 60+ countries.
WWII American servicemen provided crucial cash flow in the 1940s, ending decades of financial struggle and enabling growth.
Second-generation brothers Peter and Joe modernised operations and built winemaking excellence; third-generation David expanded internationally starting in 2001.
Babich owns 14 vineyards across Marlborough and Hawke's Bay, producing 750,000 cases annually while remaining wholly family-owned.
Fourth generation now active: David's son Peter participated in his first vintage in 2025, continuing 110-year family winemaking tradition.

MARKET.head: Franzia shifts to Australian wine in China to escape 80% U.S. tariffs
MARKET.summ: Faced with nearly 80% import taxes, one of the world's best-selling boxed wine brands has abandoned U.S.-made wine in China, replacing it with Australian wine bottled locally
takeaways: Franzia, owned by The Wine Group, now ships Australian bulk wine to China for local bottling instead of importing finished U.S. boxes due to nearly 80% total tax burden.
The brand entered China years ago with aggressive pricing—three-litre boxes under RMB100—but tariffs made that model unsustainable for affordable American wine.
Chinese products now list origin as China with Australian base wine noted separately, a shift that risks prestige but preserves Franzia's price advantage in e-commerce.
Local bottling enables faster customisation for Chinese promotions and seasonal packaging, benefits beyond tariff avoidance that the distributor says improve competitiveness.
Resumption of U.S. imports depends on tariff reduction; current policy makes American-packaged Franzia uncompetitive on Chinese shelves.

MARKET.head: Japanese award-winning wines struggle to convert accolades into export sales
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: Takahiko, a Hokkaido family winery, increased exports from 12 to 180 bottles annually after its 2017 pinot noir appeared on Noma's wine list in 2020, not awards.
Around 70 percent of Japan's 400+ wineries are family-owned operations too focused on domestic sales to pursue costly export processes and award submissions.
Kenneth Lee, a Hong Kong importer, says awards drive awareness but tastings and restaurant placements matter more for sales; he questions whether awards truly convert to revenue.
Japan's mountainous terrain requires handpicked harvests and costly special trellising, making wines expensive overseas where price remains the largest barrier to uptake.

MARKET.head: China's top supermarkets expand wine push amid sector slowdown
MARKET.summ: Here's a look at China's 10 largest supermarket chains by revenue and how each is shaping its wine strategy
takeaways: Walmart China led 2025 supermarket rankings with RMB 195.86 billion revenue; Freshippo rose to second place, both prioritizing wine via direct sourcing.
Costco China ranked 15th despite only seven stores, generating RMB 10 billion in sales, signaling wine's premium appeal in Chinese retail.
ALDI China jumped 26 places to No. 35 with 88 stores and doubled annual revenue to RMB 4 billion, partly via RMB 99 six-bottle wine bundles.
Among China's top 100 supermarkets, 52 reported declining revenue as traditional hypermarkets lose ground to value-focused direct sourcing and omnichannel rivals.

CULTURE.head: Michelin debuts Burgundy winery ratings; Arnoux-Lachaux demands removal
CULTURE.summ: The Michelin Guide has unveiled the inaugural Michelin Grape Selection, a new winery classification covering Burgundy. Using a system similar to its restaurant stars, Michelin awarded wineries one, two or three "Michelin Grapes" based on the overall quality of the estate rather than individual wines
takeaways: Michelin launched its first winery classification in Burgundy, awarding one to three 'Michelin Grapes' to 94 estates based on agronomy, technique, identity, balance and consistency.
Domaine Arnoux-Lachaux publicly rejected its one-grape rating, stating it halted wine submissions to critics in 2020 and requested removal from the guide.
Michelin evaluates entire estates rather than individual wines, emphasizing long-term consistency and vineyard management over vintage-by-vintage scoring.
The entry challenges established wine media including Wine Advocate, which Michelin acquired outright in 2019 after initially buying a 40% stake in 2017.

CULTURE.head: Champagne Barons de Rothschild releases two grand cru single-vineyard cuvées
CULTURE.summ: The latest addition to the fast-developing Rothschild Champagne house is a pair of single-site expressions from grands crus in Avize and Cramant - and Patrick Schmitt MW gets an early taste
takeaways: Les Maladries and Le Bateau, both 2021 blanc de blancs from Avize and Cramant respectively, mark the maison's second foray into single-site Champagnes.
Les Maladries uses 80-year-old vines from a 0.3-hectare plot fermented half in concrete eggs, half in barrels, with 3g/l dosage.
Le Bateau, from 50-year-old vines on pure chalk soil, ferments entirely in wood with just 2g/l dosage, yielding a very dry style.
Cellar master Guillaume Lété confirmed the series will release annually from grand cru Chardonnay vineyards owned by the maison, with 2022 vintage expected richer.

CULTURE.head: Fladgate Partnership expands beyond Port into Portuguese table wines
CULTURE.summ: The Fladgate Partnership is no longer a project just for Port - it now has wines from Bairrada, Vinhos Verdes and Dão alongside its Douro production. db explores a range informed by location as much as heritage
takeaways: The Fladgate Partnership, which owns Taylor's, Fonseca and Croft Port houses, acquired unfortified wines from Bairrada, Dão, and Vinhos Verdes starting in 2023.
The company will rebrand as Fladgate Family Wines to reflect its portfolio shift toward table and sparkling wines alongside fortified offerings.
Quinta de Bella in Dão grows Pinot Noir alongside native Touriga Nacional, with Pinot Noir showing age-worthiness comparable to Burgundy.
Quinta Colinas de São Lourenço in Bairrada produces sparkling wines and blends native varieties with international grapes in Bordeaux style.
Paço de Palmeira and Quinta da Pedra in Vinhos Verdes focus on single varietals—Loureiro and Alvarinho—proving the region's fine-wine potential.

CULTURE.head: Wine Hub integrates Wine Collector platform for private collector inventory access
CULTURE.summ: Wine Hub, a leading wine business software owned by Wine Owners, has teamed up with management and trading exchange, The Wine Collector from Nickolls & Perks
takeaways: Wine Hub, owned by Wine Owners, partnered with The Wine Collector (Nickolls & Perks) to connect professional traders with private collector wines.
Wine Hub users can now bid on and purchase bonded, professionally stored wines from private collectors directly through their trading dashboard.
The integration allows Wine Hub traders to list client consigned wines on Wine Collector Exchange and access real-time inventory transparency.
Similar to Wine Hub's existing Liv-Ex integration, the partnership aims to improve market liquidity and reduce double-selling risks for traders.
The deal follows Wine Hub's recent LiveTrade integration with Bordeaux Index, part of a broader trend toward ecosystem-building among wine industry platforms.

CULTURE.head: Mendoza winery restaurants claim two more Michelin stars in 2026 guide
CULTURE.summ: Two more winery restaurants in Mendoza have been awarded Michelin stars, further strengthening the region's reputation as one of South America's leading wine and gastronomy destinations. Winery and winemaker-led restaurants now account for almost half of Argentina's Michelin-starred establishments
takeaways: La VidA at Susana Balbo Winemaker's House and Cal at Sitio La Estocada earned Michelin stars on July 13, 2026.
Winery and winemaker-led restaurants now represent six of Argentina's 14 Michelin-starred establishments.
Mendoza dominates Argentina's starred dining scene with nine of 14 restaurants; Cal also received a Green Star for sustainability.
Chef Enzo González Petra, 28, won the Michelin Young Chef Award; sommelier Camila Torta won the Sommelier Award.
Michelin Guide Argentina launched in 2024; winery restaurants strengthen Mendoza's international wine and gastronomy reputation amid tourism recovery efforts.

CULTURE.head: English wine production surges 55% in 2025 as still wines gain ground
CULTURE.summ: Following yesterday's 2026 rankings from The Real Review's 'Top Wineries of Great Britain' tasting, in which all of the top 30 wines hailed from England, Anthony Rose analyses the current state of UK wine
takeaways: UK wine production reached 124,377 hectolitres in 2025, equivalent to 16.5 million bottles, with white wine production jumping 131% year-on-year.
Essex's Crouch Valley emerged as English still wine heartland, attracting major investments including a joint venture between Danbury Ridge and Burgundy's Domaine Duroché.
The sector now comprises 1,158 registered vineyards employing over 10,000 people, valued at £14 billion, with export volumes growing 35% in 2024.

CULTURE.head: Asia's drinks industry convenes across Hong Kong, Seoul, Tokyo in June
CULTURE.summ: From Hong Kong to Tokyo, Asia's drinks industry came together in June through education, tasting experiences and major trade events, as mixologists, sommeliers and sake makers celebrated craftsmanship, and explored new opportunities for growth
takeaways: The Bitter Truth founder Stephan Berg hosted a masterclass in Hong Kong for bartenders during the brand's 20th-anniversary Asia Tour at Bar Tincture.
Wines of Hungary held its inaugural Bor Camp in Seoul on June 23-24, bringing sommeliers and buyers together to explore Hungarian wine regions and distribution opportunities.
Sake Fair returned to Tokyo on June 19-20 with 5,600 visitors, showcasing 1,200 sakes from 45 prefectures and 400 award-winning selections from Japan Sake Awards 2026.
Lemsecco launched in China with Lemon and Blood Orange Spritz now stocked across 400+ Freshippo stores and premium chains including Ole, Apio and Citysuper.

SCIENCE.head: Half of Napa vineyards lost money last year; profitable peers pivot to hospitality
SCIENCE.summ: In WineBusiness Monthly’s annual Vineyard Survey Report, Napa Valley vintners voiced a lengthy list of concerns threatening their businesses: changing consumer preferences, succession planning, pests and diseases, labor shortages, rising farming costs, and increasingly strict regulations, to name a few.
takeaways: Napa Valley's 2024 survey found 50% of vineyards saw revenue declines and 51% experienced profit losses, threatening long-term viability.
Tasting room visits dropped since 2022 as costs soared—average tasting now $75+ plus $400+ hotel nights—forcing wineries to lower prices and offer unique experiences.
Profitable wineries prioritize direct-to-consumer channels and flexible club models with multiple membership tiers to lower entry barriers and boost retention.
Access to premium grapes like Beckstoffer and Hyde has improved due to oversupply, creating opportunity for quality-focused producers during the industry downturn.

SCIENCE.head: Grape sunburn damage spreads as heat waves reach cooler wine regions
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Sunburned grapes show sensory markers: flattened fruit, rubber, asphalt, creosote notes, plus cooked flavors, brown rims, high alcohol, and flabby acid.
Direct sun pushes berry surface temps 22–27°F above air temperature; Chardonnay shows damage within five minutes above 104–109°F under high light.
Leaf removal timing is critical: defoliation at flowering builds photoprotective compounds; defoliation at veraison or before heat waves increases sunburn risk significantly.
Vintage strategy has shifted: Chêne Bleu in Ventoux eliminated summer leaf removal after 2023 heat wave; Tyrrell's in Hunter Valley relies on kaolin since 2014.

SCIENCE.head: France's heatwave cuts grape yields across Champagne, Bordeaux, Burgundy
SCIENCE.summ: Weeks of drought are reducing yields and raising quality concerns, though weak global demand means shortages are far from certain
takeaways: Champagne expects 10% production decline; Bordeaux forecasts 20% or steeper drops due to extreme heat and drought.
Prolonged water stress during critical growth stage threatens grape quality: delayed phenolic maturity, excessive alcohol, reduced acidity.
French regions harvesting 3–4 weeks earlier than historical norms; Champagne may pick around August 15, potentially earliest on record.
Widespread oversupply in entry-level Bordeaux means lower yields unlikely to trigger shortages, but premium whites and Sauternes face steeper losses.
Some Bordeaux estates irrigating illegally to save vines; Pauillac now authorized irrigation as growers adapt to climate stress.

SCIENCE.head: Mourvèdre struggles as southern France temperatures surge past historic records
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: France recorded its highest average temperature on June 23, with harvests advancing, sugar rising, and acidity falling across southern regions.
Mourvèdre, traditionally dominant in Bandol's coastal reds and rosés, evolved from a late-ripening liability to a heat-tolerant asset as climate warmed.
Bandol producers employ canopy management, higher pruning, and ground cover to shade vines and retain soil moisture amid intensifying heat and drought.
Jumilla's vineyard surface halved in 20 years as lower-altitude sites abandoned; irrigation once rejected now deemed essential for vine survival in severe droughts.
Bandol risks shifting from 75 percent rosé production toward reds as cooler sites warm, while irrigation debates threaten the region's dry-farming identity.

SCIENCE.head: Organic Sauvignon Blanc challenges growers but rewards biodynamic farming
SCIENCE.summ: From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
takeaways: Sauvignon Blanc is difficult to grow organically because it's highly susceptible to powdery and downy mildew, requiring frequent fungicide treatment.
Jonathan Pabiot, a biodynamic winemaker in Pouilly-Fumé, says copper-based fungicides damage the vine's flowers, forcing organic growers to find alternative disease management.
Biodynamic farming helps overcome Sauvignon's climate sensitivities by building living soil, according to winemakers including Pabiot and the Bertonis of Languedoc.
Nearly 20 years of organic farming at Domaine les Eminades has made their Sauvignon notably resilient to disease in Languedoc's favorable climate.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Exploring speciality coffee and its parallels with fine wine: a visit to Fazenda Mió in Brazil to catch the harvest
NEWSLETTERS.summ: There are many parallels between speciality coffee and wine, and that’s one of the reasons I was really excited about
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Brazil produces 3.5 million tonnes of coffee annually, approximately one-third of global production.
Fazenda Mió spans 2,000+ hectares with 665 hectares of coffee plants in Minas Gerais, 200 miles north of São Paulo.
Coffee bushes yield after 3 years, are actively productive for 20 years, and Mió maintains bushes over 70 years old.
Harvest at Mió runs May to August; owners deliberately cultivate early, medium, and late varieties to spread harvest across the season.
Coffee processing—dry, washed, or semi-dry methods—is the critical stage for developing flavor compounds in the bean.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Best Wine-Related Click Bait AI Can Buy
NEWSLETTERS.summ: The allure of clickbait and reasons to support the real writers
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tom Wark
takeaways: An article titled 'The Absolute Best Winery in Every U.S. State' received more clicks than any other article in The Spill newsletter this entire year.
The story appeared designed for SEO success with clickbait headline tactics rather than traditional wine journalism.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Wine News: What I’m Reading the Week of 7/12/26
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hello and welcome to my weekly roundup of the wine stories that I find of interest on the web. I
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alder Yarrow

NEWSLETTERS.head: Notable Releases from Troon Vineyard
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Notes on a Vermentino and two Syrahs, one with some bottle age
NEWSLETTERS.author: Meg Maker
takeaways: Troon Vineyard released 2025 Vermentino and 2023 Syrah from Oregon's Applegate Valley.
Troon Vineyard, located in Grant's Pass, farms using certified biodynamic and regenerative organic principles.
Troon ferments with ambient cultures, uses minimal sulfite at bottling, and avoids new oak aging.
Applegate Valley's high latitude brings long summer days with heat, but overnight cooling from Siskiyou Mountain winds.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Is Red Wine Losing Its Grip?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: The fading appetite for structured reds, and what it says about the way we drink now
NEWSLETTERS.author: George Nordahl
takeaways: French beef consumption fell 13% and pork fell 11% between 2000 and 2021, while poultry consumption increased 31%, reducing occasions for structured, tannic reds.
Traditional French meals have become shorter, less formal, and less centered on family dining, removing a primary setting where structured red wine was served.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Veneto Wedding Traditions
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Our friends are getting married this afternoon so I got thinking about some of the things that I've discovered which have surprised me
NEWSLETTERS.author: Emma Bentley

NEWSLETTERS.head: Portland, Maine: Come Hungry
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hey, just a small catch-up after a few sweltering days at Pinewoods Dance Camp, where there was at least one day it was just too damned hot to leave Long Pond and balance and swing
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alice Feiring
takeaways: Alice Feiring recently visited Portland, Maine and recommends ordering cabbage dishes at local restaurants.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Friday 5 #186
NEWSLETTERS.summ: 4 lush white wines under £10, this month’s cookbook giveaway and where to stop for a bite that isn't Greggs
NEWSLETTERS.author: Fiona Beckett

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Calabria has nine scattered DOCs; only Cirò in the east has achieved fame, while western mountain areas remain wine-obscure.
Magliocco exists in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (eastern, more tannic) and Magliocco Canino (western, possibly Sangiovese-related, retains acidity).
Casa Comerci, certified organic since 2009 with 15 hectares of vines, grows only Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco near Nicotera on the Tyrrhenian coast.
Rosa and Michela Comerci use exclusively stainless steel fermentation and aging, avoiding barrels, yielding wines with strong fruit focus and plummy perfumes.
Cirò winery Librandi dominates coastal wine lists despite being 200km and three hours' drive north-east; artisanal producers from western Calabria remain hard to find.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Too Difficult?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Yeats entitled one of his mid-life poems ‘The Fascination of What’s Difficult’. The poem is mainly a complaint, about the tangled business of running a theatre (the Abbey in Dublin) and the infuriating difficulty of “management of men”, as opposed to the pure delight of writing poetry. But the title points to something which has
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tim Atkin MW
takeaways: Nebbiolo-based reds of Piedmont combine pale color with tannic structure and earth/smoke notes that challenge conventional expectations shaped by French red wines.
Sherry's deliberate oxidation and high acetaldehyde levels make it difficult for many drinkers, similar to how 12-tone music challenges conventional listeners.
